data.title: Is It Time To Reassess GFL Environmental (TSX:GFL) After Its Recent 12 Month Pullback?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether GFL Environmental is attractively priced at its current share price, the next sections will walk through what the numbers actually suggest.
  • The stock last closed at CA$61.51, with returns of 4.1% over the past 30 days, 4.4% year to date and a 7.3% decline over the last year, alongside longer term returns of 31.0% over 3 years and 42.1% over 5 years.
  • Recent headlines around GFL Environmental have focused on its role in commercial services and waste management, keeping attention on how the company is positioned in its sector. This backdrop helps frame whether the current share price reflects the recent 12 month pullback and multi year gains.
  • On Simply Wall St's valuation checks, GFL Environmental scores 2 out of 6. The rest of this article will compare different valuation approaches and finish with a broader way to think about whether that score really captures the full picture.

GFL Environmental scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: GFL Environmental Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to today’s value. It is essentially asking what future CA$ received over time are worth in today’s terms.

For GFL Environmental, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is about CA$209.7m. Analyst and extrapolated projections used by Simply Wall St suggest Free Cash Flow reaching CA$1.58b by 2030, with a series of annual estimates in between, all converted into today’s value using a discount rate.

Adding these discounted cash flows together results in an estimated intrinsic value of CA$100.86 per share. Compared to the recent share price of CA$61.51, this implies an intrinsic discount of about 39.0%, which indicates that the shares appear undervalued on this DCF view alone.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests GFL Environmental is undervalued by 39.0%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 8 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for GFL Environmental.

Approach 2: GFL Environmental Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to think about valuation because it links the price you pay directly to the earnings the business is currently generating. Higher growth expectations and lower perceived risk often justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually go with a lower, more conservative P/E range.

GFL Environmental currently trades on a P/E of 105.0x. That is above the Commercial Services industry average P/E of 17.1x and also above the peer group average of 70.2x. On the face of it, that points to a richer valuation compared with both the broader industry and closer comparables.

Simply Wall St’s “Fair Ratio” concept goes a step further. The Fair Ratio of 35.6x is a proprietary estimate of what GFL Environmental’s P/E might be based on factors such as its earnings growth profile, profit margins, industry, market cap and specific risks. Because it incorporates these company level characteristics, it can be more tailored than simple comparisons with peers or industry averages. Comparing the current P/E of 105.0x with the Fair Ratio of 35.6x indicates that the shares are trading above the level this framework views as more balanced.

Result: OVERVALUED

Approach 1: BOK Financial Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a company is expected to generate above the return that equity investors require, and then attributes that surplus to each share.

For BOK Financial, the starting point is its book value of US$97.63 per share and a stable book value estimate of US$109.25 per share, based on future book value estimates from 7 analysts. The model also uses a stable EPS estimate of US$10.50 per share, sourced from weighted future return on equity estimates from 6 analysts, together with an average return on equity of 9.61%.

The cost of equity is set at US$8.68 per share. On this basis, the model estimates an excess return of US$1.82 per share above that requirement. Aggregating these expected excess returns and discounting them back gives an intrinsic value estimate of roughly US$149.30 per share under the Excess Returns framework.

Compared with the recent share price of US$135.72, the model implies the stock is around 9.1% undervalued, which sits in the margin-of-error zone for this type of analysis.

data.title: Is Freshworks (FRSH) Starting To Look Interesting After A 36.8% Share Price Slide?
data.description:
• Wondering if Freshworks at US$7.94 is starting to look interesting or still has more room to fall before it feels like value.
  • The stock has seen a 4.6% decline over the last 7 days and is down 31.6% year to date, with a 36.8% decline over the past year that may have changed how the market views its risk and reward profile.
  • Recent headlines around Freshworks have focused on its position within the broader software sector and shifting sentiment toward growth oriented names. This helps explain some of the pressure on the share price. Investors are watching how the company responds to this backdrop, including any updates on its product focus, customer trends, or cost discipline.
  • Freshworks currently scores 4 out of 6 on a set of valuation checks. The next sections will walk through what different valuation methods say about the stock, before finishing with a way to look at value that goes beyond the usual ratios and models.

Find out why Freshworks's -36.8% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Approach 1: Freshworks Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes estimates of a company’s future cash flows and discounts them back to today to arrive at an implied value per share. It is essentially asking what those future dollars are worth in today’s money.

For Freshworks, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flow projections. The latest twelve month free cash flow is around $225.1 million, with analyst and extrapolated estimates pointing to free cash flow of $326.9 million in 2028 and further projections out to 2035. Simply Wall St uses analyst inputs where available, then extends the forecast path to build a 10 year cash flow stream.

When those cash flows are discounted back, the resulting intrinsic value from this DCF comes out at about $24.37 per share. Against the recent share price of $7.94, this implies a 67.4% discount, which points to Freshworks trading materially below this estimate of fair value.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Freshworks is undervalued by 67.4%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 62 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Approach 2: Freshworks Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to link what you pay today with the earnings the business is already generating. It gives you a simple snapshot of how many dollars investors are paying for each dollar of earnings.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on how the market views a company’s growth prospects and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher risk usually calls for a lower one.

Freshworks currently trades on a P/E of 12.28x. That sits below both the Software industry average of 27.81x and the peer group average of 43.51x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Freshworks is 4.66x, which is its proprietary view of what a reasonable P/E might be after accounting for factors such as earnings growth, industry, profit margins, market cap and risk profile.

The Fair Ratio is more tailored than a plain industry or peer comparison because it adjusts for the specific characteristics of Freshworks rather than assuming all software names should trade on similar multiples. Comparing 12.28x to the 4.66x Fair Ratio suggests the shares are trading above this customised benchmark.

Result: OVERVALUED

Meet Narratives, a simple tool on Simply Wall St’s Community page that lets you attach a story to your numbers by linking your view of Freshworks’ products, AI competition and customer mix to a forecast for revenue, earnings and margins. The Excess Returns model looks at how efficiently a bank turns its equity base into earnings after covering the required return that shareholders expect. Instead of focusing on cash flows, it centers on return on equity and what that implies for long term value.

For Fifth Third Bancorp, the model starts with a Book Value of $30.18 per share and a Stable EPS estimate of $4.75 per share, based on weighted future return on equity estimates from 11 analysts. That leaves an Excess Return of $2.16 per share, which is the economic profit the bank is estimated to generate above its equity cost.

The Stable Book Value is put at $37.19 per share, using weighted future book value estimates from 13 analysts. When these excess returns are projected and capitalized, the model arrives at an intrinsic value of about $97.69 per share, compared with the recent share price around $50.22, which implies the stock is 48.6% undervalued on this framework.
